subject: Physics
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation with stopping potentials:
For first light, $h u_1 = \phi + eV_{s1}$.
For second light with $\lambda_2 = 2\lambda_1$ (so $ u_2 = u_1/2$): $h u_1/2 = \phi + eV_{s2}$.
From given values: $h u_1 - 3.2e = h u_1/2 - 0.7e$ gives $h u_1/2 = 2.5e$.
Thus $\lambda_1 = rac{hc}{5e} = rac{6.63 imes 10^{-34} imes 3 imes 10^8}{5 imes 1.6 imes 10^{-19}} \approx 2.5 imes 10^{-7}$ m.

solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{hv}=\phi+\mathrm{KE}_{\max } \\
& \mathrm{KE}_{\max }=\mathrm{eV}_0 \\
& \mathrm{~V}_0=\frac{\mathrm{hv}-\phi}{\mathrm{e}}
\end{aligned}$
(A) $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \mathrm{V}$ is linear correct
(B) Slope
$\mathrm{v}_0=\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}}\right) \mathrm{v}-\frac{\phi}{\mathrm{e}} \text { Wrong }$

Slope $\frac{h}{e}$
(C) Correct
(D) Incorrect
(E) Correct

subject: Physics
solution:
Einstein's photoelectric equation
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{KE}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi_0 \\
& 2 \mathrm{eV}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-1 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \frac{h c}{\lambda}=3 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \mathrm{KE}^{\prime}=\frac{h c}{(\lambda / 2)}-\phi_0=6 \mathrm{eV}-1 \mathrm{eV}
\end{aligned}$
$=5 \mathrm{eV}$

subject: Physics
solution:
(A) Stopping potential depends on both frequency of light and work function.
(B) Saturation current ∝ intensity of light.
(C) Maximum KE depends on frequency.
(D) Photoelectric effect is explained using particle theory. In this phenomenon, when electromagnetic radiation (such as light) hits the material, the emission of electrons takes place. In the photoelectric effect, if the frequency is too low, no electron is seen getting freed. But, if the frequency is high enough, some electrons can be observed. These observations prove that the Light is made of particles. The energy of the particle increases with the frequency.

subject: Physics
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \Phi + eV_0$

The energy of the incident photon is:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{331 \times 10^{-9}}$

$E = \dfrac{19.86 \times 10^{-26}}{331 \times 10^{-9}} = 6 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is:

$K_{\text{max}} = eV_0 = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.2 = 0.32 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The work function $\Phi$ is:

$\Phi = E - K_{\text{max}}$

$\Phi = 6 \times 10^{-19} - 0.32 \times 10^{-19} = 5.68 \times 10^{-19}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-19}$ J, we get $\alpha = 5.68$.

Answer: $5.68$

For electron

Also λp=λe. Thus,

hcEp=hc8Ke⇒KeEp=18

subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the proton is given by

λp=h2mpqpV   ...1

The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the alpha particle is given by

λα=h2mαqαV'   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

λpλα=mαmp×qαV'qpV   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

λpλα=41×2×41×2=4:1

subject: Physics
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $h u = W + KE$.
With zero kinetic energy, $h u = W$.
The frequency is $ u = rac{W}{h} = rac{110 imes 10^{-20}}{6.63 imes 10^{-34}} = 16.59 imes 10^{14}$ Hz.
The angular frequency is $\omega = 2\pi u = 2\pi imes 16.59 imes 10^{14}$
$= 104.25 imes 10^{14} = 1.04 imes 10^{16}$ rad/s.

subject: Physics
solution:
The de-Broglie's wavelength is given by λ=hmv, where, h is Planck's constant and v is velocity.

Using relation between momentum and kinetic energy, we have λ=h2 mKE

Here, kinetic energy of electron is stated as KE=qV.

Then, we have λ=h2mqV

Putting the values of m=9.1×10-31 kg and q=1.62×10-19 C of electron

We get, λ=1.22V nm.

So, x stand for V.

subject: Physics
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}}$.
For a deuteron (mass $m_d = 2m_p$) with energy $E$: $\lambda_d = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 2m_p \cdot E}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{4m_p E}}$
For an alpha particle (mass $m_\alpha = 4m_p$) with energy $2E$: $\lambda_\alpha = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 4m_p \cdot 2E}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{16m_p E}}$
The ratio: $\frac{\lambda_d}{\lambda_\alpha} = \frac{\sqrt{16m_p E}}{\sqrt{4m_p E}} = \sqrt{4} = 2$

subject: Physics
solution:
Energy of photon $=E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$
$\Rightarrow$ Wavelength of photon $=\lambda=\frac{h c}{E}$
Energy of proton $=E=\frac{1}{2} m_p v^2=\frac{P^2}{2 m_p}$
$\Rightarrow$ Linear momentum of proton $=P=\sqrt{2 m_p E}$
Or de-Broglie wavelength of proton
$\begin{aligned}
=\lambda_p= & \frac{h}{P}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p E}} \\
\text { Ratio } \frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda} & =\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p E}} \times \frac{E}{h c} \\
& =\frac{1}{c} \sqrt{\frac{E}{2 m_p}}
\end{aligned}$

Energy of a single photon E=hcλ.

Intensity is defined as power per unit area. Therefore,

Intensity of incident beam I=EtA

If the number of photons crossing the given area per second is n, then

n=Enet Esingle pholon =IAλhc

⇒n=1001×10-4900×10-96.62×10-34×3×108=4.5×1016.

solution: Frank-Hertz's experiment demonstrated the existence of excited states in mercury atoms helping to confirm the quantum theory which predicted that electrons occupied only discrete quantized energy states. The photo-electric experiment demonstrated that photon is the particle of light, which can transfer momentum and energy due to collision. Davidson-Germer's experiment shows the wave nature of electrons.
